steps.title: Find the narrator's emphasis
steps.content: The narrator begins with a self-deprecating description, but that is not where the passage's main focus stays. He quickly shifts to the truly great man he knew, describing that person's natural genius for invention. The narrator stresses that he spent years beside him, watched him work, and witnessed his ideas take shape.

steps.title: Choose the broadest match
steps.content:
• Choice A - 'A person was fortunate enough to have found love despite his obvious shortcomings.': The reference to the narrator's wife is only part of his self-description, not the central idea.
  • Choice B - 'An exceptionally talented person professes humility to avoid intimidating others.': The humble language comes from the narrator, not from the talented person he admires.
  • Choice D - 'A truly brilliant person lets ideas mature in his mind before expressing them aloud.': This focuses too narrowly on invention and leaves out the narrator's long experience observing someone he deeply admired.
The remaining choice best states the narrator's appreciation for the time he spent with the admired man. The answer is C.
